title: Inequalities For Trees and Matroids
speaker: Federico Ardila
abstract:
In their 1971 study of telephone switching circuitry, Graham and Pollak designed a novel addressing scheme that was better suited for the faster communication required by computers. They introduced the distance matrix of a graph, and used its eigenvalues to bound the size of the addresses in their scheme. We continue to study distance matrices coming from trees, and use their spectral properties to obtain new inequalities for trees, matroids, and valuated matroids.
This is joint work with Graham Denham, Chris Eur, June Huh, and Botong Wang.

proof_stages.label: Distance matrices and smashed-cube addressings
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from the Graham--Pollak distance matrix of a graph and the problem of realizing graph distances by zero-one-star addresses of minimum length, motivating spectral questions about such matrices.

proof_stages.label: Quadratic-form spectral lower bound for address length
proof_stages.summary: An addressing of length n rewrites the distance-matrix quadratic form as a difference of n positive and n negative squares, so Sylvester-type inertia bounds force n to be at least the maximum of the numbers of positive and negative eigenvalues.

proof_stages.label: Graham--Pollak tree theorem via determinant signs
proof_stages.summary: For a tree, an elementary construction gives addresses of length |V|-1, while the tree distance matrix has determinant and principal-minor signs, read in pruning order, implying exactly one positive and |V|-1 negative eigenvalues and hence optimality.

proof_stages.label: Metric leaf distance matrices
proof_stages.summary: The discussion passes to metric trees with positive edge lengths and to the leaf distance matrix B_T, which remains Lorentzian by principal-submatrix and limiting arguments, with full and leaf distance matrices related by adding zero-length leaves.

proof_stages.label: Sharp all-ones shift for rooted equidistant trees
proof_stages.summary: For a rooted equidistant metric tree, the negative leaf distance matrix becomes positive semidefinite after a height-scaled rank-one shift by the all-ones matrix with sharp constant 1-1/n.

proof_stages.label: Matroid and valuated-matroid inequalities to be proved
proof_stages.summary: Mason-type log-concavity conjectures for numbers of independent sets are upgraded to coefficientwise multivariate and q-weighted valuated-matroid inequalities, identifying the combinatorial statements that the new matrix inequality is meant to support.

proof_stages.label: Lorentzian-polynomial formulation
proof_stages.summary: Using the recursive definition of strictly Lorentzian homogeneous polynomials via Lorentzian Hessians and derivatives, the main matroid statement is packaged as Lorentzianity of a multivariate q-weighted independent-set polynomial, whose specializations imply the stated conjectures.

proof_stages.label: Recursive reduction to rank-two tree metrics
proof_stages.summary: The Lorentzian proof reduces under x-derivatives to matroid contractions and under y-derivatives to rank-two valuated matroids, which by the phylogenetic four-point theorem are tree metrics, so the needed Hessian positivity is exactly the sharp leaf-distance matrix theorem.

examples.name: Bell-system telephone network graph
examples.note: The historical telephone-routing network is used as a running graph on which to display a distance matrix, attempted zero-one-star addresses, and later a leaf submatrix; it motivates the definitions but does not supply a proof step.

examples.name: Triangle obstruction to zero-one cube embeddings
examples.note: The triangle graph shows that ordinary zero-one Hamming cubes cannot realize all graph distances, visibly motivating the introduction of the starred or smashed-cube addresses.
examples.engagement_reason: the triangle is only cited as an obstruction because cubes have no triangles, with no computation or construction carried out on the triangle.

examples.name: Graphic matroid and forest interpretation
examples.note: Edges of a graph with acyclic subgraphs illustrate matroids, and in the Q&A the coefficientwise inequality is rephrased as comparing ways to split an edge set into two forests of different sizes.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker specializes the matroid inequalities to graphic matroids by interpreting independent sets as forests and explaining what the coefficient counts mean in terms of splitting edge sets into forests.

examples.name: Equidistant star tree in the Q&A
examples.note: An audience member tests the sharp rank-one shift theorem on the extreme tree whose leaf distances are all equal, clarifying the role of the all-ones matrix and the constant.
examples.engagement_reason: in the Q&A the equidistant star tree is analyzed by identifying its leaf-distance matrix, eigenvector/eigenvalue behavior, and the effect of the all-ones shift.
